coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of phosphate in human serum with a metal-organic frameworks-based nanocomposite and its immobilized agarose hydrogels
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Nan
Huang, Jian
Wang, Liyuan
Feng, Jiayu
Huang, Pengcheng
Wu, Fangying
coredata.dc:description: We report a metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)-based nanocomposite by incorporating a fluorescent dye, rhodamine B (RhB), into a stable Zr-based MOF, UiO-66-NH2. Based on weakened the LMCT process resulted from the strong binding of phosphate with Zr-O node in UiO-66-NH2 and the unchangable fluorescence of RhB as the reference, this nanocomposite can be employed as a novel fluorescent sensing platform for ratiometric detection of phosphate in the range from 80 to 400 µM with a detection limit of 2.0 µM. It is particularly suitable for accurate quantification of phosphate in complex samples like human serum. Furthermore, this sensing platform is well recyclable due to reversible interaction with phosphate. Finally, this nanocomposite was encapsulated into agarose hydrogels for direct detection of phosphate in human serum with the naked eye. Thanks to the robust, portable, and instrument-free characteristics, it is expected to show great potential for on-site clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Phosphate detection
Metal-organic frameworks
Agarose hydrogel

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phosphate detection using RhB@UiO-66-NH2 immobilized agarose hydrogels
Mechanism of fluorescent response to phosphate
Fluorescence detection of phosphate in human serum using RhB@UiO-66-NH2 in solution
Visual detection of phosphate in human serum using RhB@UiO-66-NH2 immobilized agarose hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PXRD patterns of RhB, simulated UiO-66, synthesized UiO-66-NH2 and RhB@UiO-66-NH2. (b) FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2 (i), RhB@UiO-66-NH2 (ii), RhB@UiO-66-NH2 + phosphate (iii) and RhB (iv). The SEM images of (c) UiO-66-NH2 and (d) RhB@UiO-66-NH2.
(a) Emission spectra of UiO-66-NH2, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 and RhB. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of UiO-66-NH2, RhB and RhB@UiO-66-NH2 in the absence and presence of phosphate.

(a) Emission spectra of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 in the absence and presence of phosphate. Inset: the photograph of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 suspension in the absence (left) and presence of 900 µM phosphate (right) under a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) The SEM image of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence of phosphate.

(a) Emission spectra of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 responding to phosphate of different concentrations. Inset: the photograph of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 suspension in the presence of phosphate of different concentrations (left to right: 0, 50, 100, 300, 600, 900 µM) under a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Fitting plot of I430nm/I585nm versus phosphate concentration in the range from 80 to 400 µΜ.

(a) The fluorescence intensity ratio (I430nm/I585nm) of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence of different potentially interfering substances (400 µM). (b) Reversibility of phosphate sensing of RhB@UiO-66-NH2.

(a) The linear calibration curve between I430nm/I585nm of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 agarose hydrogels and phosphate concentration in the range of 0.5–10 mM. Inset: the corresponding photograph for phosphate of different concentrations (left to right: 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 mM) under a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Emission spectra of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 agarose hydrogels before and after incubated in human serum samples. Inset: the corresponding photograph under a 365 nm UV lamp.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 and the possible mechanism for ratiometric detection of phosphate.
Results for detection of phosphate in human serum samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration values shown in the table are the extrapolated concentrations of the original serum.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of phosphate in human serum with a metal-organic frameworks-based nanocomposite and its immobilized agarose hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)-based nanocomposite by incorporating a fluorescent dye, rhodamine B (RhB), into a stable Zr-based MOF, UiO-66-NH2. Based on weakened the LMCT process resulted from the strong binding of phosphate with Zr-O node in UiO-66-NH2 and the unchangable fluorescence of RhB as the reference, this nanocomposite can be employed as a novel fluorescent sensing platform for ratiometric detection of phosphate in the range from 80 to 400 µM with a detection limit of 2.0 µM. It is particularly suitable for accurate quantification of phosphate in complex samples like human serum. Furthermore, this sensing platform is well recyclable due to reversible interaction with phosphate. Finally, this nanocomposite was encapsulated into agarose hydrogels for direct detection of phosphate in human serum with the naked eye. Thanks to the robust, portable, and instrument-free characteristics, it is expected to show great potential for on-site clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphates, including lipid phosphates, inorganic phosphates, and phosphate esters, play an important role in biological systems as one of the well-established essential nutrients needed for gene construction, signal transduction, energy storage, and bone mineralization [1–4]. Particularly, inorganic phosphate is one of the most important electrolytes, which are critical to maintain the body's phosphate balance and homeostasis by participating in most metabolic processes and enzymatic reactions [5,6]. The understanding of phosphate levels in body fluids like human serum, can provide useful information for medical diagnosis of several diseases. For example, the lack of phosphorus in the body can lead to low immunity and various diseases; however, the excess phosphate in blood is associated with hyperphosphatemia and cardiovascular complications [7,8]. Hence, it is vital to quantitatively determine phosphate in body fluids.

Although many fluorescent analytical methods have been developed to detect phosphate owing to their advantages including easy operation, high sensitivity, and great potential in remote control, traditional organic molecules-based fluorescent probes are generally employed in homogenous detection systems, which substantially restricts practical applications since they have difficulties in their separation for recycling and poor water solubility for analysis in aqueous solution [9–14]. To improve the efficiency of fluorescent sensing, various fluorescent sensors in heterogeneous systems have been recently designed, especially using inorganic materials [15–23]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), constructed by coordination between metal ions/clusters and organic ligands, have tunable porosity, high specific surface area, and controllable compositions [24,25]. In the past decade, MOFs have attracted extensive attention as potential candidates for sensing materials due to excellent luminescent property in addition to the above attractive characteristics [26,27]. Specifically, MOFs can effectively increase target concentrations to higher levels, thereby enhancing host-guest interactions to output analytical responses. To date, several MOFs-based fluorescent sensing platforms have been established for the detection of phosphate [28–32]. However, most of them are still problematic. For example, their structures usually collapsed when phosphates were introduced, thus impairing their reusability [30–32]. Moreover, these probes are rarely applied in complicated body fluids presumably because they are based on a single fluorescence change, which is vulnerable to external environment especially considering the complexity of the biological sample matrix [28–31]. Finally, the reported solution-phase sensing is not suited to construct a robust solid sensing platform for facile, quick, portable, and on-site analysis of phosphate in clinical and biological samples. Therefore, it is highly desirable to achieve simple and reliable detection of phosphate in human serum with an advanced sensory format.

Chemicals were obtained from commercial sources. Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, >99.5%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin) Chemical Co. Ltd. 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), dichloromethane (DCM), rhodamine B (RhB), 4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), and various ions (Na3PO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, CaCl2, KCl, NaCl, NaBr, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Ca(NO3)2) were bought from China pharmaceutical group chemical reagent Co. Ltd. Amino acids including phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), methionine (Met), glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys), arginine (Arg), lysine (Lys), leucine (Leu), serine (Ser), valine (Val), isoleucine (Ile), and histidine (His), and agarose were purchased from Shanghai Jingchun Technology Co. Ltd. All reagents are directly used in the experiment without any purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1).

The fluorescence spectra were performed on a F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp source and a 1.0 cm quartz cell. UV–vis absorption spectra were conducted at room temperature on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). A FEI Quanta200F Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope (ESEM, NLD) was used to characterize the size and morphology of MOFs. Powder X-Ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) were recorded on Bruker AXS D8 (BRUKER.AXS, DEU) and the data were collected within the 2θ range of 5–40°. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were detected on Nicolet 380 FT-IR Spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) at room temperature.

UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized according to the synthesis method reported previously [34]. Typically, ZrCl4 (84.8 mg, 0.363 mmol) and ATA (65.6 mg, 0.363 mmol) were dissolved in DMF (40 mL) at room temperature and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was then sealed in a Teflon reactor (50 mL) and maintained at 120 °C for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained yellow precipitate was centrifuged and washed with DMF to remove the unreacted ligands and metals and dried at room temperature under reduced pressure. Before using the MOFs, the occluded DMF molecules were eliminated by repeated washing with DCM, followed by drying under vacuum (100 °C, 12 h).

Briefly, 2 mg of UiO-66-NH2 was dispersed in RhB aqueous solutions (4 mL, 2 mM) and sonicated for 2 h in a dark environment. Then, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water several times. Whether the unadsorbed RhB is completely removed was determined by monitoring the absorbance of the supernatant (Fig. S1). The powder was collected and re-dispersed in HEPES (4 mL, 0.1 M, pH 7.4). The RhB@UiO-66-NH2 stock suspension was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.08.092.

Briefly, 2 mg of UiO-66-NH2 was dispersed in RhB aqueous solutions (4 mL, 2 mM) and sonicated for 2 h in a dark environment. Then, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water several times. Whether the unadsorbed RhB is completely removed was determined by monitoring the absorbance of the supernatant (Fig. S1 ). The powder was collected and re-dispersed in HEPES (4 mL, 0.1 M, pH 7.4). The RhB@UiO-66-NH2 stock suspension was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

First, 50 µL of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 stock suspension was diluted with HEPES (0.1 M, pH 7.4) to a volume of 500 µL. Then, 10 µL phosphates at different concentrations were separately added into the above suspension of RhB@UiO-66-NH2. After incubated for 2 h, fluorescence spectra were measured with the 357 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phosphate detection using RhB@UiO-66-NH2 immobilized agarose hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: RhB@UiO-66-NH2 immobilized in agarose hydrogels was prepared according to our previous report [35]. Generally, 0.25 g of agarose powder was dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water and heated by a water bath until it is completely dissolved. Then, 4 mL RhB@UiO-66-NH2 (0.2 mg/mL) was added into the above solution and stirred well. After that, the solution was transferred into a 96-well plate, which acted as a mold. Finally, the hydrogels naturally cooled to room temperature. In order to detect phosphate, the hydrogels were immersed in 1.0 mL HEPES (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing phosphates at different concentrations. After 1 h incubation, the hydrogels were then removed from the solution and dried for the next emission spectra.

Human serum was obtained from healthy volunteers at Nanchang University Hospital. In general, proteins were removed by mixing human serum (0.5 mL) and acetonitrile (2.0 mL) in a centrifuge tube. After vortex-mixing, the sample was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min, and then the supernatant was transferred into a 25.0 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with ultrapure water.

For fluorescence detection of phosphates in human serum usng RhB@UiO-66-NH2 suspension, phosphates at different concentrations were spiked with 10 µL of the human serum samples, and subsequently added into RhB@UiO-66-NH2 suspension. Finally, the emission spectra were recorded.

For the visual detection of phosphates in serum with RhB@UiO-66-NH2 immobilized agarose hydrogels, the hydrogels were directly soaked into the undiluted serum sample. The following procedures were similar to those mentioned above.

The synthesized UiO-66-NH2 was characterized by PXRD (Fig. 1 a). Obviously, it exhibited similar peaks to the simulated ones of UiO-66 [34]. After adsorbing RhB, the XRD pattern changed little, and meanwhile, the characteristic diffraction peaks of RhB were not observed. This indicates that the adsorption of RhB did not affect the lattice of UiO-66-NH2 presumably because RhB was embedded in the cavity rather than physically adsorbed on the surface due to the unique micropore structure of UiO-66-NH2 [36]. Fig. 1b compared the FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and RhB@UiO-66-NH2. It was revealed that the absorption band at 483 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration peak of Zr-O and the absorption peaks at 1434 cm−1 assigned to CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group in ATA [29] showed no clear shift with and without RhB adsorption for UiO-66-NH2. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of RhB were not found at 1589, 1180, 1345, and 1645 cm−1, further demonstrating that RhB was entrapped in the pore of UiO-66-NH2 and the vibration of RhB was hindered due to the shielding effect of the three dimentional structure of UiO-66-NH2 [37]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 1c and d) also displayed that the morphology and size of UiO-66-NH2 had a slight change before and after RhB (Fig. S2).

The synthesized UiO-66-NH2 was characterized by PXRD (Fig. 1 a). Obviously, it exhibited similar peaks to the simulated ones of UiO-66 [34]. After adsorbing RhB, the XRD pattern changed little, and meanwhile, the characteristic diffraction peaks of RhB were not observed. This indicates that the adsorption of RhB did not affect the lattice of UiO-66-NH2 presumably because RhB was embedded in the cavity rather than physically adsorbed on the surface due to the unique micropore structure of UiO-66-NH2 [36]. Fig. 1b compared the FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and RhB@UiO-66-NH2. It was revealed that the absorption band at 483 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration peak of Zr-O and the absorption peaks at 1434 cm−1 assigned to CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group in ATA [29] showed no clear shift with and without RhB adsorption for UiO-66-NH2. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of RhB were not found at 1589, 1180, 1345, and 1645 cm−1, further demonstrating that RhB was entrapped in the pore of UiO-66-NH2 and the vibration of RhB was hindered due to the shielding effect of the three dimentional structure of UiO-66-NH2 [37]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 1c and d) also displayed that the morphology and size of UiO-66-NH2 had a slight change before and after RhB (Fig. S2).

The optical properties of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 were next investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 430 nm and 585 nm, which corresponded to UiO-66-NH2 and RhB, respectively. The peak positions shifted a little compared with individual UiO-66-NH2 and RhB. This difference may arise from their interaction in the nanocomposite. Very importantly, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm ascribed to the emission of the ligand ATA was much lower than that of free ATA (Fig. S3). This phenomenon can be explained from the following two aspects: one is the LMCT process between ATA and Zr-O clusters [29], and the other is the solvent-induced fluorescence quenching effect where the guest molecules of H2O in the cavities would affect the electron transfer of ATA and the Zr-coordination sphere via weak hydrogen bonds by coupling with the Zr-O nodes [38,39]. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited a wide absorption band in the range of 300–450 nm with an absorption peak about 370 nm (Fig. 2b) similar to UiO-66-NH2, which was attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of the aromatic ring [39]. Besides, two characteristic absorption peaks of RhB emerged in the range of 495–596 nm after the adsorption, which also confirmed that RhB was successfully adsorbed on UiO-66-NH2. Taken together, the above results well demonstrate the efficient formation of the nanocomposite through adsorption of RhB into UiO-66-NH2.

The optical properties of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 were next investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 430 nm and 585 nm, which corresponded to UiO-66-NH2 and RhB, respectively. The peak positions shifted a little compared with individual UiO-66-NH2 and RhB. This difference may arise from their interaction in the nanocomposite. Very importantly, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm ascribed to the emission of the ligand ATA was much lower than that of free ATA (Fig. S3). This phenomenon can be explained from the following two aspects: one is the LMCT process between ATA and Zr-O clusters [29], and the other is the solvent-induced fluorescence quenching effect where the guest molecules of H2O in the cavities would affect the electron transfer of ATA and the Zr-coordination sphere via weak hydrogen bonds by coupling with the Zr-O nodes [38,39]. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited a wide absorption band in the range of 300–450 nm with an absorption peak about 370 nm (Fig. 2b) similar to UiO-66-NH2, which was attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of the aromatic ring [39]. Besides, two characteristic absorption peaks of RhB emerged in the range of 495–596 nm after the adsorption, which also confirmed that RhB was successfully adsorbed on UiO-66-NH2. Taken together, the above results well demonstrate the efficient formation of the nanocomposite through adsorption of RhB into UiO-66-NH2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescent response to phosphate
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 at 430 nm significantly enhanced after phosphate was added, while the emission from RhB at 585 nm did not change obviously. It can also be seen that the fluorescence color of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 under UV light changed from orange red to blue. From this observation, we infer that fluorescent response of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 to phosphate was mainly caused by strong interaction between Zr-O clusters and phosphate and there is no interaction between phosphate and RhB. In this case, such interaction weakened the LMCT process as described above and consequently led to the fluorescence recovery of ATA.

To gain insight into this mechanism for phosphate detection, a series of experiments were carried out to exploit the interaction between phosphate and the framework of RhB@UiO-66-NH2. FT-IR spectra of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 were measured in the presence and absence of phosphate (Fig. 1b). After phosphate was added, a wide absorption peak appeared at 1092 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1, which corresponded to the asymmetrical stretching vibrations of PO bonds [29]. This indicates that phosphate molecules were successfully anchored onto the framework of RhB@UiO-66-NH2. In addition, the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl group in RhB@UiO-66-NH2 shifted to 1438 cm−1, respectively. This shift demonstrates more robust coordination of Zr–O nodes with phosphate than with the carboxyl group in ATA, which essentially weakened the interaction between Zr–O nodes and ATA to make the carboxyl group with stronger vibration. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2b, the UV–vis spectrum of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 showed a large blue-shift of the absorption peak around 370–323 nm after the addition of phosphate. The same trend was also verified for the UV–vis spectrum of UiO-66-NH2 upon addition of phosphate (Fig. S4). However, the absorption peak of RhB did not shift greatly. These results also suggest that the interaction between the Zr-O nodes and phosphate weakened the LMCT process. Besides, according to the SEM image in Fig. 3b, the morphology of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 did not change significantly in the absence and presence of phosphate. This implies that the addition of phosphate only weakened the interaction of Zr-O nodes and ATA without the structural damage of RhB@UiO-66-NH2.

To gain insight into this mechanism for phosphate detection, a series of experiments were carried out to exploit the interaction between phosphate and the framework of RhB@UiO-66-NH2. FT-IR spectra of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 were measured in the presence and absence of phosphate (Fig. 1b). After phosphate was added, a wide absorption peak appeared at 1092 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1, which corresponded to the asymmetrical stretching vibrations of PO bonds [29]. This indicates that phosphate molecules were successfully anchored onto the framework of RhB@UiO-66-NH2. In addition, the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl group in RhB@UiO-66-NH2 shifted to 1438 cm−1, respectively. This shift demonstrates more robust coordination of Zr–O nodes with phosphate than with the carboxyl group in ATA, which essentially weakened the interaction between Zr–O nodes and ATA to make the carboxyl group with stronger vibration. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2b, the UV–vis spectrum of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 showed a large blue-shift of the absorption peak around 370–323 nm after the addition of phosphate. The same trend was also verified for the UV–vis spectrum of UiO-66-NH2 upon addition of phosphate (Fig. S4). However, the absorption peak of RhB did not shift greatly. These results also suggest that the interaction between the Zr-O nodes and phosphate weakened the LMCT process. Besides, according to the SEM image in Fig. 3b, the morphology of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 did not change significantly in the absence and presence of phosphate. This implies that the addition of phosphate only weakened the interaction of Zr-O nodes and ATA without the structural damage of RhB@UiO-66-NH2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of phosphate in human serum using RhB@UiO-66-NH2 in solution
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best conditions for the detection of phosphate, the concentration of RhB was first investigated. With the increasing concentration of RhB, the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio at 430 and 585 nm, ΔI430nm/I585nm, before and after the addition of phosphate gradually increased and reached a plateau at 2.0 mM (Fig. S5). The impact of pH on detection sensitivity of the probe was examined. As shown in Fig. S6, as the pH value increased, the probe's response to phosphate increased first and then decreased, reaching a highest sensitivity around 7.4, which is very close to physiological pH and suitable for biological samples. It is noted that the ratio value sharply decreased at pH 7.0 and 8.5, so HPO4 2− is considered as the sensing substance (Fig. S7). The time-dependent change of I430nm/I585nm of the probe in the presence of phosphate was finally studied. It was found that I430nm/I585nm gradually increased over the range of 0–110 min and reached a steady value after 120 min (Fig. S8).

To obtain the best conditions for the detection of phosphate, the concentration of RhB was first investigated. With the increasing concentration of RhB, the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio at 430 and 585 nm, ΔI430nm/I585nm, before and after the addition of phosphate gradually increased and reached a plateau at 2.0 mM (Fig. S5). The impact of pH on detection sensitivity of the probe was examined. As shown in Fig. S6, as the pH value increased, the probe's response to phosphate increased first and then decreased, reaching a highest sensitivity around 7.4, which is very close to physiological pH and suitable for biological samples. It is noted that the ratio value sharply decreased at pH 7.0 and 8.5, so HPO4 2− is considered as the sensing substance (Fig. S7). The time-dependent change of I430nm/I585nm of the probe in the presence of phosphate was finally studied. It was found that I430nm/I585nm gradually increased over the range of 0–110 min and reached a steady value after 120 min (Fig. S8).

Under the optimized conditions, the sensitivity of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 toward phosphate was examined. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the increase of phosphate concentration, the fluorescence intensity of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 at 430 nm gradually increased, while the emission peak of RhB at 585 nm varied slightly. Along with this, the color of the solution under UV lamp changed from orange red to blue (inset in Fig. 4a), which may provide convenient identification with the naked eye for phosphate at different concentrations. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. 4b, there is a good linear relationship between I430nm/I585nm and the concentration of phosphate in the range of 80–400 µM. The linear equation is y = 0.344 x + 24.346 with a regression coefficient (R = 0.991) where y was the value of I430nm/I585nm and x was the concentration of phosphate. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.0 µM (S/N = 3).

Serum is a very complex system, so high selectivity of the established method is required. To evaluate the selectivity of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 toward phosphate, we tested the responses toward the potential interfering substances coexisting in serum, including metal ions, anions, and amino acids. As shown in Fig. 5 a, in the presence of these substances, no significant ratiometric fluorescence response was observed, strongly indicating excellent selectivity toward phosphate.

In particular, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited good reversibility in phosphate detection based on robust coordination with phosphate of the competitor Eu3+ ions to regenerate the probe. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, in each cycle, I430nm/I585nm of RhB@UiO-66-NH2 was increased by the addition of phosphate to switch on the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2. After adding Eu3+ ions, I430nm/I585nm was almost restored to the blank level since the enhanced fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2 fell into its initial value. These results demonstrated that the probe was well recyclable in phosphate detection thus offering a significant advantage over some existing MOFs-based sensors in which MOFs are usually not reusable mainly because of the loss of structural integrity when phosphate is introduced [30–32].

Owing to the excellent sensitivity and selectivity and built-in correction for environmental effects demonstrated above, we next assessed the practicality of the RhB@UiO-66-NH2 as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for phosphate quantitation in human serum. As listed in Table 1 , the phosphate level in human serum was detected at 2.87 mM. In addition, to further confirm the accuracy of our presented approach, a standard addition method was applied. Satisfying recoveries were obtained in the range of 98%–102%, demonstrating that this assay is highly accurate and reliable for the detection of phosphate in the complicated biological matrix like human serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of phosphate in human serum using RhB@UiO-66-NH2 immobilized agarose hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: To facilitate wider practical application of the proposed approach, RhB@UiO-66-NH2 was immobilized into agarose hydrogels to construct a robust solid sensing platform for visual detection of phosphate in human serum simply with the naked eye.

Hydrogels are three-dimensional macromolecular networks of hydrophilic polymers cross-linked through noncovalent or covalent interactions [35,40–44]. They possess many attractive physical properties such as good biocompatibility, high porosity, large loading capacities, and adjustable shapes. Consequently, hydrogels can provide an ideal environment for accommodating guest molecules, and such hybrid materials will combine the advantages of hydrogels with those of guest molecules showing potential for chemical and biological applications. Moreover, hydrogels are very useful for developing the visual detection platform especially considering their negligible background colors and fluorescence emissions [35,40–43].

Agarose hydrogels, which are thermo reversible, were chosen as the model system, being low-cost and easily crosslinked under mild conditions by hydrogen bond linking [35,40–44]. The porous structure of agarose would also facilitate the diffusion process of the analyte through the gel matrix. The nanocomposite RhB@UiO-66-NH2 can be easily incorporated within the hydrogels by virtue of hydrogen bonding between the amine groups in UiO-66-NH2 and hydroxyl groups on the hydrogel network. To evaluate the analytical performance of the RhB@UiO-66-NH2-immobilized agarose hydrogels for phosphate detection, the hydrogels were incubated into phosphate solutions at various concentrations. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2 at 430 nm was sensitive to phosphate and increased gradually with the increment of phosphate concentration, while a negligible change was observed for the fluorescence intensity of RhB at 585 nm. It is gratifying that a good linear correlation was still obtained between I430nm/I585nm and phosphate concentration in the range of 0.5–10 mM (Fig. 6 a). Besides, under a UV lamp, the fluorescence color of the hydrogels turned blue from orange red accordingly (inset in Fig. 6a), which is consistent with the change in solution.

Agarose hydrogels, which are thermo reversible, were chosen as the model system, being low-cost and easily crosslinked under mild conditions by hydrogen bond linking [35,40–44]. The porous structure of agarose would also facilitate the diffusion process of the analyte through the gel matrix. The nanocomposite RhB@UiO-66-NH2 can be easily incorporated within the hydrogels by virtue of hydrogen bonding between the amine groups in UiO-66-NH2 and hydroxyl groups on the hydrogel network. To evaluate the analytical performance of the RhB@UiO-66-NH2-immobilized agarose hydrogels for phosphate detection, the hydrogels were incubated into phosphate solutions at various concentrations. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2 at 430 nm was sensitive to phosphate and increased gradually with the increment of phosphate concentration, while a negligible change was observed for the fluorescence intensity of RhB at 585 nm. It is gratifying that a good linear correlation was still obtained between I430nm/I585nm and phosphate concentration in the range of 0.5–10 mM (Fig. 6 a). Besides, under a UV lamp, the fluorescence color of the hydrogels turned blue from orange red accordingly (inset in Fig. 6a), which is consistent with the change in solution.

Finally, the RhB@UiO-66-NH2-immobilized agarose hydrogels were utilized to demonstrate the practical applicability for phosphate detection in human serum. A noticeable fluorescence color change occurred before and after incubated in serum sample (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, based on the corresponding fluorescence spectrum, the phosphate concentration in the sample was calculated to be 2.69 mM according to the linear relationship in Fig. 6a, which is in good agreement with the data measured with RhB@UiO-66-NH2 solution. Therefore, this robust and portable hydrogels may offer a reliable and facile way to realize the visual detection of phosphate levels in human serum for on-site analysis.

We designed a new ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe, RhB@UiO-66-NH2, for accurate, selective and sensitive detection of phosphate. In this approach, UiO-66-NH2 could bind strongly with phosphate to weaken the LMCT process, giving rise to the fluorescence recovery of the ligand, while RhB was only used as the internal reference to prevent the influence from external factors, which makes it very potential to visualize the concentration-dependent fluorescence color changes even in practical samples including human serum. Furthermore, this nanoprobe could exhibit reversible responses toward phosphate based on competitive interaction with Eu3+ ions. Finally, the RhB@UiO-66-NH2 nanocomposite was immobilized into agarose hydrogels to allow facile visual detection of phosphate in human serum. We thus believe that this nanocomposite is very promising as a potent fluorescent nanoprobe for phosphate detection in related medical diagnosis.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21505067, 21765014).
